children.name: Cissopis
children.description: The magpie tanager — a monotypic genus that is the largest tanager in the Neotropics. Its striking black-and-white plumage and long, graduated tail give it a magpie-like appearance, hence the name. It is a social bird of forest edges and clearings, often found in noisy groups.

children.name: Cissopis leverianus
children.description: The largest tanager, reaching 30 cm in length, with bold black-and-white plumage and a long, graduated tail. It ranges across much of South America east of the Andes, from Colombia to northern Argentina. It is highly social and often encountered in small flocks that move noisily through the mid-story of lowland forests, occasionally descending to fruiting shrubs.

children.name: Sericossypha
children.description: The white-capped tanager — a monotypic genus of striking highland tanagers found only in the Andean cloud forests. Its most distinctive feature is the brilliant white crown that contrasts sharply with its otherwise deep black and blue plumage. These large, gregarious tanagers travel in tight-knit groups through the canopy.

children.name: Sericossypha albocristata
children.description: A spectacular large tanager with a snowy white crown, deep black body, and cobalt-blue shoulders and rump. It lives in small, tight-knit flocks in the canopy of Andean cloud forests from Venezuela to Peru at 1,500 to 3,000 metres. Its far-carrying, whistled contact calls help the flock stay together as it travels through the misty canopy.

children.name: Buthraupis
children.description: The mountain tanagers — robust, large-bodied tanagers of the high Andes. They have stout bills and brightly coloured plumage in vivid combinations of blue, yellow, and green. These bold tanagers inhabit elfin forest, páramo scrub, and Polylepis woodland at the highest elevations of any tanager.

children.name: Buthraupis montana
children.description: A stunning high-Andean tanager with a yellow body, black hood, and bright blue wings and tail. It is found in cloud forests and elfin woodlands from Venezuela to Bolivia at elevations of 2,000 to 3,800 metres. It travels in noisy flocks, often following mixed-species foraging groups through the mossy canopy.

children.name: Chlorornis
children.description: The grass-green tanager — a monotypic genus whose single species is one of the most vividly coloured of all tanagers. Its entire body is a brilliant, uniform grass green with striking rufous and black accents on the face and throat. Found only in the temperate forests of the high Andes.

children.name: Chlorornis riefferii
children.description: A uniformly brilliant grass-green tanager with a rufous throat and lower belly, and black around the bill and eye. It inhabits humid montane forests of the Andes from Colombia to Bolivia at elevations of 1,800 to 3,300 metres. It is typically seen in pairs or small groups, foraging for fruit and insects in the moss-laden branches of cloud forests.

children.name: Sporathraupis
children.description: The blue-capped tanager — a genus of medium-sized, highland tanagers with a distinctive bright blue cap that contrasts with their otherwise greenish-olive plumage. They are birds of the Andes, inhabiting cloud forest edges and secondary growth where they travel in small, active flocks.

children.name: Sporathraupis cyanocephala
children.description: A handsome highland tanager with a bright blue crown, greenish-olive upperparts, and grey underparts. It ranges from Venezuela to Bolivia in Andean cloud forests at elevations of 1,500 to 3,800 metres. It is typically seen in small flocks, often accompanying mixed-species foraging parties in the canopy.

children.name: Chalcothraupis
children.description: The golden-naped tanager — a monotypic genus of strikingly coloured tanagers found in the Andes. The male's plumage is a brilliant combination of golden nape, black mask, and blue body, making it one of the most eye-catching tanagers in its range. It inhabits humid montane forests.

children.name: Chalcothraupis ruficervix
children.description: A brilliantly coloured tanager with a golden nape and throat, a black face and breast, and a turquoise-blue body. It is found from Colombia to Bolivia in cloud forests at 1,000 to 2,500 metres. It is a lively, active bird that travels in mixed-species flocks, often hanging upside-down while gleaning insects from mossy branches.

children.name: Tangara
children.description: The Tangara tanagers — a vast, colourful genus of small to medium-sized tanagers that are the crown jewels of the Neotropical bird world. Their plumage exhibits an extraordinary range of iridescent blues, greens, yellows, reds, and purples, often in complex multicoloured patterns. They are active, social birds of the forest canopy, constantly on the move in noisy flocks.

children.name: Tangara gyrola
children.description: A beautiful tanager with a rich chestnut head and throat, bright green body, and blue-green wings. It ranges from Costa Rica through the Amazon basin to northern Bolivia and southern Brazil. It is a common resident of humid lowland forests, often seen in small groups moving through the canopy in search of fruit.

children.name: Tangara arthus
children.description: A brilliant golden-yellow tanager with black wings and tail edged in green. It ranges across the northern Andes from Venezuela to Bolivia, inhabiting humid montane forests at elevations of 500 to 2,500 metres. It is a canopy specialist, often seen in mixed-species flocks where its golden plumage flashes in the sunlight.

children.name: Tangara icterocephala
children.description: A striking tanager with a gleaming silvery-white throat, bright yellow underparts, and black-streaked green upperparts. It is found from Costa Rica to Ecuador in humid foothill and montane forests. It travels in active flocks through the mid-story and canopy, often associating with other tanager species at fruiting trees.

children.name: Tangara parzudakii
children.description: A spectacular tanager with a brilliant red and yellow face, a black crown, and a blue-green body. It is found from Colombia to Peru in montane cloud forests at 1,000 to 2,500 metres. The face pattern varies geographically, with northern birds having more red and southern birds more yellow on the face.

children.name: Tangara seledon
children.description: A dazzling tanager with a bright green head, blue throat and belly, yellow-orange breast band, and orange rump. It is endemic to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil, Paraguay, and northeastern Argentina. It is one of the most striking members of the genus, often visiting fruit feeders at forest lodges.

children.name: Tangara cyanocephala
children.description: A colourful tanager with a bright blue crown, red nape and throat, green back, and blue underparts. It is found in the Atlantic Forest of eastern Brazil, from Bahia to Rio Grande do Sul. It inhabits humid lowland and montane forests, often joining mixed-species flocks at fruiting trees in the canopy.

children.name: Tangara cyanicollis
children.description: A beautiful tanager with a brilliant blue head and throat, a green body, and black wings and tail edged in blue. It ranges from Colombia and Venezuela to Peru and Bolivia in foothill and montane forests. It is usually found in small, active flocks that move quickly through the canopy, making them challenging to observe but stunning when seen.

children.name: Tangara chilensis
children.description: One of the most spectacularly coloured of all birds, with a light green head, blue belly, red rump, and multicoloured wings. It ranges across the Amazon basin from Colombia to Bolivia and Brazil. Despite its vivid plumage, it can be surprisingly hard to spot as it moves through the dark canopy, but at fruiting trees its colours are breathtakingly vivid.

children.name: Tangara velia
children.description: A subtly stunning tanager with a deep blue body, black face, and a spectacular opalescent rump that shifts from green to gold to pale blue depending on the light. It is found in the Amazon basin and the Guianas, preferring lowland terra firme and várzea forests. It is typically seen in pairs or small groups in the canopy.

children.name: Tangara mexicana
children.description: A brilliant turquoise-blue tanager with a yellow belly and black face and throat. Despite its scientific name, it does not occur in Mexico — its range spans the Amazon basin, the Guianas, and eastern Brazil. It is a gregarious bird of forest edges and clearings, often found in small flocks that move excitedly among fruiting trees.

children.name: Tangara cyanotis
children.description: A striking tanager with a vivid blue eyebrow, black mask, and bright green body with bluish underparts. It ranges from Colombia to Bolivia in humid montane forests at elevations of 1,000 to 2,500 metres. It is typically found in pairs or small groups in the canopy, often accompanying mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Tangara lavinia
children.description: A handsome tanager with warm rufous wing coverts, a green body, and a pale blue belly. It ranges from Honduras to Colombia and Ecuador in lowland and foothill humid forests. It is a canopy dweller, often seen in small flocks foraging for fruit in the upper branches of tall trees.

children.name: Tangara punctata
children.description: A small green tanager heavily spotted with black on the breast and head, found across the Amazon basin and the Guianas. Its spotted pattern is unique among Tangara and makes it easily identifiable. It inhabits lowland rainforest, often in mixed-species flocks that move through the canopy feeding on fruit and insects.

children.name: Tangara guttata
children.description: A green tanager with fine black speckling on the breast and a golden-yellow crown bordered by black. It ranges from Costa Rica to the Amazon basin and the Guianas. It is a bird of humid lowland and foothill forests, where it travels in small flocks in the canopy, often at fruiting trees.

children.name: Tangara varia
children.description: A small olive-green tanager with fine dark spots on the breast and throat, found in the Guianas and adjacent Brazil. It is one of the more subtly coloured Tangara species, but the delicate spotting gives it a charming appearance. It inhabits lowland rainforest and is often seen in small groups in the canopy.

children.name: Tangara rufigula
children.description: A beautiful tanager with a distinctive rufous throat and breast, a green crown and back, and blue-green wings. It is found in the Andes of Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru at elevations of 1,000 to 2,300 metres. It inhabits cloud forest edges and secondary growth, often in pairs or small flocks.

children.name: Tangara nigroviridis
children.description: A stunning tanager named for the brilliant beryl-blue spangles on its black breast and belly. Its upperparts are green and its crown is blue, creating a dazzling effect in good light. It ranges from Venezuela to Bolivia in Andean cloud forests at 1,500 to 3,200 metres.

children.name: Tangara johannae
children.description: A spectacular tanager with a bright blue cheek patch or 'whisker', a black face, and a brilliant green body with yellow belly. It is found in the lowlands of western Colombia and northwestern Ecuador. It inhabits humid lowland forests and is often seen in mixed-species flocks in the canopy.

children.name: Tangara schrankii
children.description: A beautiful tanager with bright golden-yellow underparts, a green crown and back, and a black mask around the bill. It ranges across the western Amazon basin from Colombia to Bolivia. It is a common resident of humid lowland forests, often seen in flocks at fruiting trees in the canopy.

children.name: Tangara florida
children.description: A brilliant emerald-green tanager with a yellow belly and a black patch around the eye. It ranges from Costa Rica to western Ecuador in humid lowland and foothill forests. Despite its vivid colouration, it can be surprisingly inconspicuous as it moves through the dark canopy in pairs or small groups.

children.name: Tangara viridicollis
children.description: A striking tanager with a silvery-grey back, blue wings, black tail, and a bright yellow belly. It is found in the Andes of Peru and Bolivia at elevations of 1,000 to 2,800 metres. It inhabits cloud forest and humid montane forest edges, often in small flocks in the mid-story and canopy.

children.name: Thraupis
children.description: The typical tanagers — medium-sized, robust birds of Neotropical forests and gardens. They have stout bills adapted for a mixed diet of fruit and insects, and are often among the most common tanagers in their range. Their plumage is predominantly blue or grey-blue, with some species showing bright wing markings.

children.name: Thraupis episcopus
children.description: One of the most widespread and familiar tanagers of the Neotropics, ranging from Mexico to the Amazon. Its soft blue-grey plumage with white wing patches makes it easily recognisable in gardens, parks, and forest edges. It is highly adaptable and thrives in human-altered landscapes, often visiting fruit feeders at lodges and homes throughout its range.

children.name: Thraupis palmarum
children.description: An olive-green tanager with a slight yellowish wash, closely associated with palm trees across much of South America. It is typically encountered in small groups in palm groves, gardens, and open woodland east of the Andes. Its subdued colouration distinguishes it from most other tanagers, but its habit of perching prominently on palm fronds makes it easy to observe.

children.name: Thraupis sayaca
children.description: A soft blue-grey tanager with paler underparts and faint whitish wing patches, very similar to the Blue-grey Tanager but with a greener cast to the back. It is common across eastern and central South America, from Brazil south to Argentina. It adapts readily to urban parks and gardens, where its subdued beauty and simple song make it a familiar companion.

children.name: Thraupis ornata
children.description: A handsome blue-grey tanager with bright yellow patches on the wing coverts that form a golden 'chevron'. It is endemic to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil. It inhabits humid forest, secondary growth, and gardens, often in pairs or small groups, and is a regular visitor to fruit feeders.

children.name: Piranga
children.description: The piranga tanagers — a genus of brightly coloured American birds formerly classified with true tanagers but now placed within the cardinal family, though they remain in Thraupidae by tradition in many treatments. Males are brilliant red, yellow, or orange; females are yellowish-olive. They are birds of forest canopy and woodland edges, gleaning insects and taking fruit.

children.name: Piranga rubra
children.description: The only entirely red bird in North America — males are a uniform rose-red with no wing bars or crest. It breeds in open deciduous woodlands of the southern and eastern United States and winters from Mexico to the Amazon basin. Uniquely among North American tanagers, it specialises in catching bees and wasps, often beating them against a branch to remove the stinger before swallowing.

children.name: Piranga olivacea
children.description: One of the most striking North American songbirds: the male is brilliant scarlet with jet-black wings and tail. It breeds in mature deciduous forests of eastern North America and undertakes a remarkable migration to the northern Andes, where it moults into a subdued greenish plumage. Its hoppy, robin-like song is a familiar sound of eastern woodlands in summer.

children.name: Piranga ludoviciana
children.description: The western counterpart of the Scarlet Tanager, with the male showing a bright yellow body, black back, wings, and tail, and a red face. It breeds in coniferous and mixed forests from the Pacific coast to the Rocky Mountains and winters from Mexico to Central America. Its slow, burry song resembles that of a robin with a sore throat.

children.name: Ramphocelus
children.description: The glossy tanagers — a genus of strikingly coloured Neotropical birds with a distinctive enlarged, silvery lower mandible that gives them their name. Males are among the most brilliantly hued of all tanagers, showing deep crimson, velvety black, and glossy blue. They are active, conspicuous birds of forest edges and secondary growth, often in small groups.

children.name: Ramphocelus carbo
children.description: A common and widespread tanager across much of northern South America and the Amazon basin. The male is velvety crimson-black with a striking silvery-blue lower mandible that is the basis for its common name. It is highly social, often found in noisy groups at forest edges and in second-growth woodland, and is a regular visitor to fruit feeders.

children.name: Ramphocelus bresilius
children.description: One of the most brilliantly coloured tanagers, endemic to coastal Brazil. The male is an intense crimson-red with a glossy silver-white bill and black wings and tail. It inhabits restinga scrub, forest edges, and gardens along the Atlantic Forest from Paraíba to Santa Catarina. Its vivid colouration makes it a flagship species for Atlantic Forest conservation.

children.name: Pipraeidea
children.description: The fawn-breasted tanager — a small genus of tanagers found in South America, with a range spanning the Andes and the southern cone. Their plumage is characterised by soft fawn, blue, and yellow tones, a subtle elegance compared to the brilliant colours of other tanagers. They inhabit open woodland, savanna, and forest edges.

children.name: Pipraeidea melanonota
children.description: A subtly beautiful tanager with a fawn-brown breast, blue-grey head, and bright blue wings and tail. It ranges from Venezuela to Argentina along the Andes and in southeastern South America. It inhabits open woodlands, savanna, and scrub, where it forages for fruit and insects in pairs or small family groups.

children.name: Rauenia
children.description: The blue-and-yellow tanager — a monotypic genus found in southern South America. Its combination of blue, yellow, and olive plumage is distinctive among southern cone tanagers. It is a bird of open and semi-open habitats, often encountered in pairs or small groups in the temperate woodlands of the region.

children.name: Rauenia bonariensis
children.description: A handsome tanager with a blue head and breast, yellow belly, and olive-green back. It is found from Bolivia and Brazil south to central Argentina, inhabiting open woodland, scrub, and agricultural areas. The male is more brightly coloured than the female, and its cheerful presence is a highlight of the temperate southern cone landscapes.

children.name: Sicalis
children.description: Yellow-finches — a genus of small, seed-eating tanagers that resemble true finches in appearance and behaviour. They are birds of open grasslands, savannas, and agricultural areas, with yellow and olive plumage often showing considerable sexual dimorphism. Their stout conical bills are adapted for crushing seeds on the ground or in low vegetation.

children.name: Sicalis flaveola
children.description: A brilliant yellow finch-like tanager with an orange crown and a stout seed-cracking bill. It is common across much of South America in open country, gardens, and parks, from Colombia to Argentina. It has been introduced to Hawaii, Puerto Rico, and several other islands, where it thrives in disturbed habitats. Its cheerful, canary-like song makes it a popular cage bird.

children.name: Volatinia
children.description: The grassquits — a small, monotypic genus of finch-like tanagers represented by a single widespread species. These tiny, seed-eating birds are characteristic of grassy areas and agricultural land throughout the Neotropics. Males are glossy blue-black in breeding plumage, performing a distinctive jumping display flight while singing.

children.name: Volatinia jacarina
children.description: A tiny seed-eating tanager that resembles a small bunting. The breeding male is glossy blue-black with white underwing linings that flash during its remarkable display flight: it leaps vertically from a perch, flutters its wings, and sings a buzzy note on the descent, like a grasshopper taking flight. It is abundant in weedy fields and pastures from Mexico to Chile.

children.name: Coereba
children.description: The bananaquits — a distinctive, monotypic genus of small, active tanagers with a curved bill adapted for nectar feeding. They are among the most familiar and widespread birds of the Neotropics, found from the Bahamas to Brazil. Their behaviour and morphology so resemble those of true honeycreepers that they were once placed in their own family.

children.name: Coereba flaveola
children.description: One of the most widespread and variable passerines in the Neotropics, with 41 recognised subspecies. It has a curved, pointed bill ideal for nectar feeding, a white eyebrow stripe, and a yellow breast and rump. A habitual nectar-robber, it pierces flower bases with its bill to access nectar without pollinating. It is a common visitor at sugar feeders across the Caribbean and Central and South America.

children.name: Tersina
children.description: The swallow tanager — a monotypic genus unique among tanagers for its deeply notched, swallow-like tail and aerial foraging behaviour. It is morphologically distinct from other tanagers, with a flattened bill and a habit of capturing insects on the wing in open areas. Its unusual features have long puzzled taxonomists, who have variously placed it in its own family.

children.name: Tersina viridis
children.description: A unique, aerially adapted tanager with a notched tail and flattened bill, reminiscent of a swallow. The male is a stunning turquoise-blue with a black face patch; the female is green. It ranges from Panama to northern Argentina, inhabiting forest edges and clearings where it hawks insects in sustained flight. It nests in cavities, often in arboreal termite nests.

children.name: Schistochlamys
children.description: The black-faced tanagers — a small genus of Neotropical tanagers restricted to South America. They are characterised by their relatively plain, grey or olive plumage contrasted with a dark blackish face mask. These shy, unobtrusive birds inhabit forest edges and savanna-like habitats, foraging in pairs or small groups for insects and fruit.

children.name: Schistochlamys melanopis
children.description: A distinctive tanager of South America's open habitats, with a blue-grey body, whitish underparts, and a bold black face mask covering the lores and ear coverts. It inhabits savanna, cerrado, and scrubland from Colombia east to the Guianas and south to Bolivia and Brazil. It is often encountered in pairs, foraging low in bushes and small trees.

children.name: Anisognathus
children.description: The mountain tanagers — a genus of robust, colourful tanagers found in the high Andes. Their name means 'unequal jaw', referring to the slightly notched lower mandible. They are boldly patterned in combinations of yellow, blue, black, and red, making them some of the most spectacular birds of the cloud forest canopy.

children.name: Anisognathus lacrymosus
children.description: A striking mountain tanager with a yellow body, black mask and wings, and a distinctive blue 'teardrop' mark below the eye that gives it its name. It ranges from Venezuela to Peru in Andean cloud forests at elevations of 1,800 to 3,400 metres. It travels in noisy flocks through the canopy, often with other tanager species.

children.name: Anisognathus igniventris
children.description: A spectacular tanager with a brilliant scarlet belly and rump, a black head and back, and bright blue wings. It is found from Peru to northwestern Argentina at elevations of 2,500 to 4,000 metres. It inhabits elfin forest and Polylepis woodland, often in small flocks that forage actively for fruit and insects.

children.name: Anisognathus somptuosus
children.description: A gorgeous tanager with a yellow body, black mask, and brilliant blue wings and tail. It ranges from Venezuela to Bolivia in humid montane forests at 1,000 to 3,000 metres. It is a lively, active bird that travels in groups through the canopy, often accompanying mixed-species foraging flocks.

children.name: Iridosornis
children.description: The golden-collared tanagers — a small genus of brilliantly coloured Andean tanagers named for the Greek 'iris' (rainbow) and 'ornis' (bird). They are characterised by a striking golden or yellow patch on the nape or throat, contrasting with their otherwise deep blue or purple plumage. They inhabit high-elevation cloud forests.

children.name: Iridosornis rufivertex
children.description: A beautiful tanager with a bright golden-yellow crown, a deep blue body, and a rufous patch at the nape. It ranges from Colombia to Peru in Andean cloud forests at elevations of 1,800 to 3,400 metres. It travels in small, tight-knit flocks in the canopy, often visiting fruiting trees and epiphytes.

children.name: Conirostrum
children.description: The conebills — small, active tanagers with slender, pointed bills adapted for gleaning insects from foliage. They are among the smallest tanagers, often likened to warblers in their behaviour and appearance. Most species have blue-grey or greenish upperparts and pale underparts, and they travel in restless mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Conirostrum cinereum
children.description: A small, greyish conebill with a whitish eyebrow and pale underparts, found in the Andes from Colombia to Chile and Argentina. It inhabits open high-altitude scrub, Polylepis woodland, and agricultural areas at elevations of 2,000 to 4,500 metres. It is an active forager, constantly flicking its wings while gleaning insects from leaves and branches.

children.name: Conirostrum sitticolor
children.description: A striking conebill with a bright blue back, black crown and nape, and white underparts. It ranges from Venezuela to Bolivia in Andean cloud forests at elevations of 1,500 to 3,500 metres. It is a restless, active bird that travels in mixed-species flocks, gleaning insects from mossy branches and leaves in the canopy.

children.name: Conirostrum albifrons
children.description: A colourful conebill with a white forecrown, blue-grey upperparts, and bright yellow underparts. It is found from Colombia to Bolivia in Andean cloud forests at 1,500 to 3,000 metres. The male is more brightly coloured than the female, and both sexes travel in mixed-species flocks in the canopy.

children.name: Diglossa
children.description: The flowerpiercers — a distinctive genus of tanagers with an unusual hooked upper mandible that is adapted for piercing flower bases to access nectar. Unlike hummingbirds, they do not pollinate the flowers they feed from but instead 'rob' the nectar through the hole they pierce. They are active, acrobatic birds of montane forests and scrublands.

children.name: Diglossa lafresnayii
children.description: A large, glossy blue-black flowerpiercer with a distinctive hooked bill tip. It is found in the high Andes from Venezuela to Peru at elevations of 2,500 to 4,200 metres. It inhabits cloud forest edges, elfin woodland, and páramo scrub, where it aggressively defends flowering shrubs from other nectar-feeding birds.

children.name: Diglossa humeralis
children.description: A sooty-black flowerpiercer with a greyish shoulder patch that is visible in good light. It ranges from Colombia to Peru in Andean highlands at elevations of 2,000 to 4,000 metres. It is a common bird of shrubby pastures, forest edges, and secondary growth, where it pierces flowers with its specialised bill.

children.name: Diglossa sittoides
children.description: A unique flowerpiercer with rusty-rufous underparts and blue-grey upperparts, quite different in appearance from the predominantly black Diglossa species. It ranges from Venezuela to Argentina along the Andes and also in the highlands of eastern Brazil. It inhabits dry montane scrub and open woodland, piercing flowers for nectar.

children.name: Chlorophonia
children.description: The chlorophonias — plump, short-tailed tanagers with brilliant green and blue or yellow plumage. They are closely related to the euphonias and were formerly placed in their own family. Their stout bills and fruit-heavy diet make them important seed dispersers in Neotropical forests. Males are among the most vividly coloured of all tanagers.

children.name: Chlorophonia cyanea
children.description: A stunning small tanager with a bright green body, brilliant blue nape and rump, and a yellow belly. It occurs in scattered populations from Colombia to Argentina and also in the Atlantic Forest of Brazil. It inhabits humid montane and lowland forests, often foraging for fruit in the canopy and at forest edges.

children.name: Chlorophonia pyrrhophrys
children.description: A beautiful chlorophonia with a bright green body, a chestnut-brown breast patch, and a blue belly. It ranges from Colombia to Peru in Andean cloud forests at elevations of 1,500 to 3,000 metres. It is a shy, inconspicuous bird that stays in the canopy, often detected only by its soft, whistled calls.
